stories.headline: Colorado Wildfire Update: Aspen Acres reaches 34% containment as critical fire weather returns this weekend
stories.summary:
Firefighters increased containment on the Aspen Acres fire to 34% as of Saturday, up from 28% the day prior, while the blaze grew by less than a square mile overnight to 97,505 acres in Pueblo County. Operations Section Chief Brad Washa cautioned that the fire is a "long duration event" and that command will soon transfer to a new incident management team as a standard rotation measure.

All four of Colorado's major active wildfires — Aspen Acres, Ferris, Gold Mountain and Willow — remain under mandatory evacuation orders and have collectively burned 200,976 acres, or 314 square miles. Crews are particularly focused on the southwestern edge of the Aspen Acres fire near Camp Jackson and are working to prepare homes along the southern edge above the town of Rye.

Firefighters face record-high temperatures of 90 to 100 degrees, single-digit humidity and wind gusts up to 40 mph this weekend. The Ferris fire in Dolores County stands at 63,271 acres and 19% containment, while the Gold Mountain fire near Ouray dropped to 6% containment after renewed growth on its northeastern edge.

stories.headline: Aspen Acres fire decimates Beulah Valley water infrastructure; residents face bottled-water advisory upon return
stories.summary:
Local water district officials walking the Beulah Valley this week counted roughly 70 homes destroyed by the Aspen Acres fire and posted bottled-water advisories on properties throughout the unincorporated Pueblo County community. Charissa Fryberger, administrator for the Pine Drive Water District, said the situation exposed gaps in Colorado's emergency water-notice rules, which require door postings within 24 hours — a system not designed for communities where homes have been incinerated.

The fire burned all four watersheds that supply Beulah's year-round population of approximately 500. Water managers had already been bracing for shortages heading into summer after a record-dry winter ended a month early, and they had drawn up contingency plans to share resources between the valley's two main water systems.

The added uncertainty from the fire now complicates those plans significantly. Officials say restoring clean water service will be a lengthy process, and the full extent of damage to distribution infrastructure is still being assessed.

stories.headline: Water World evacuated after phone threat; Federal Heights police find no credible danger
stories.summary:
The Federal Heights Police Department responded to a "potential threat" at Water World, the large water park at 8801 N. Pecos St. in north metro Denver, forcing a full evacuation of guests around noon Saturday. After a thorough search, police announced at 3:30 p.m. that "there was no credible threat found within the park and there is no ongoing threat to the public."

Park officials said guests who left belongings behind could retrieve them Saturday until 7 p.m. via the 90th Avenue entrance, or starting at 8 a.m. Sunday through the Learn-to-Swim Pool entrance near the red brick administration building. Water World said it plans to reopen Sunday and will share information about ticket reimbursements at a later date.

stories.headline: Rockies select Kentucky shortstop Tyler Bell with 10th overall pick in MLB Draft
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies used the 10th overall pick in Saturday's MLB Draft to select Tyler Bell, a switch-hitting shortstop from the University of Kentucky who is considered the top sophomore-eligible prospect in the country. Bell, 6-foot-1 and 190 pounds, bypassed professional baseball two years ago when the Rays drafted him 66th overall out of high school, choosing instead to raise his draft stock in the SEC.

His decision paid off despite a difficult season. Bell dislocated his left shoulder and tore his labrum in his non-throwing arm on the first game of the year, yet returned to hit .343 with nine home runs over 41 games while posting a .974 fielding percentage at shortstop. He is slotted to receive a signing bonus of $6,393,100 as the 10th pick.

Bell is the second consecutive shortstop taken by Colorado at the top of the draft, following Ethan Holliday — son of Rockies legend Matt Holliday — who went fourth overall in 2025. Bell said he had his best pre-draft meeting with the Rockies and called the organization "the right fit."

stories.headline: Rockies beat Giants 4-3 on Kyle Karros broken-bat single in ninth inning
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies defeated the San Francisco Giants 4-3 Friday night on a broken-bat, two-run single by Kyle Karros during a three-run ninth inning. Antonio Senzatela earned the win, improving to 9-1 with a 3.00 ERA, while Giants starter Kyle Kilian dropped to 2-5.

The victory opened what is now a series between the two clubs, with both teams sitting near the bottom of the National League standings. Colorado entered the game at 39-57, while San Francisco stood at 39-55.

stories.headline: Nuggets waive center Jonas Valanciunas before guarantee deadline, saving at least $8 million
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets have waived center Jonas Valanciunas ahead of his contract guarantee deadline, a move that saves the franchise at least $8 million. The decision reflects the organization's ongoing effort to manage salary and avoid deeper luxury-tax exposure during an unsettled offseason.

The Nuggets continue to pursue star free agents, including LeBron James, though that pursuit is considered a longshot. The team's Summer League squad opened play in Las Vegas with a 97-86 loss to the Rockets, with new additions making their debuts alongside returning guard KJ Simpson.

stories.headline: Falling Denver rents are drawing lower-income renters out of subsidized housing and into market-rate units
stories.summary:
A glut of newly built apartments is pushing Denver rents down approximately 8% compared with 2025, back to levels last seen five years ago, according to the monthly Apartment Insights report from Denver-based Apartment Appraisers & Consultants. The vacancy rate in the seven-county Denver area peaked around 7.6% after an unprecedented delivery of 23,000 new units in 2024, creating conditions in which landlords are offering as many as 10 to 12 weeks of free rent to attract tenants.

The falling rents are producing an unintended consequence for the affordable-housing sector: Some lower-income renters who hold subsidized units are opting to move into conventional market-rate apartments that may offer nicer finishes and fewer bureaucratic requirements. Analyst Scott Rathbun said the annual income-recertification paperwork required to maintain affordable-unit eligibility is more burdensome than many people realize, adding to the appeal of simply renting on the open market.

The shift is creating complications for city and state agencies that subsidize construction of income-restricted housing. If subsidized projects see higher-than-expected vacancy, it can affect the financial viability of those buildings and the public programs that funded them.

stories.headline: Colorado homeowners insurance now sixth most expensive in U.S., up 61% since 2023
stories.summary:
Colorado homeowners are paying an estimated average of $4,164 in annual premiums in 2026, ranking the state sixth most expensive in the nation and representing a 61% increase since 2023, according to insurance-comparison site Insurify. The national average stands at $3,057, up 26% in the same period; Colorado's cumulative increase since 2019 has more than doubled at 100.8%, the largest such increase in the country.

State Insurance Commissioner Michael Conway acknowledged existing programs — including forthcoming grants for fortified roofs and the state's FAIR plan for high-risk property owners — are not sufficient on their own. "The market isn't going to solve them by itself," Conway said, calling for innovative approaches and welcoming outside ideas.

Industry analysts attribute the surge partly to construction costs, which are up 42% per square foot since before the pandemic, as well as ongoing wildfire and hail risk. Colorado's FAIR plan currently covers only a couple hundred properties, and officials say the goal is to eventually make the private market robust enough that the backstop plan is unnecessary.

stories.headline: Colorado primary analysis: Democratic Socialists ascend, Republican nominees face long odds in November
stories.summary:
A Colorado Sun analysis of this year's primary results finds the Democratic Socialist wing of the Democratic Party emerging as a dominant force in state politics, highlighted by Melat Kiros' upset defeat of 15-term U.S. Rep. Diana DeGette and state Sen. Julie Gonzales' surprisingly strong showing against incumbent U.S. Sen. John Hickenlooper, in which Gonzales captured more than 47% of the vote despite being vastly outspent.

Columnist Mario Nicolais draws a parallel between Kiros' victory and Lauren Boebert's 2020 primary upset of Rep. Scott Tipton, arguing both candidates recognized a voter anger that entrenched incumbents failed to detect. He suggests Gonzales, who did not burn political bridges in her Senate run, could emerge as a future threat to Denver Mayor Mike Johnston or Sen. Michael Bennet.

On the Republican side, Nicolais argues the party nominated largely unelectable candidates for statewide office in a state that has trended decisively toward Democrats in recent cycles. The analysis positions Colorado as one of the most ideologically split congressional delegations in the country heading into November.

stories.headline: Two Belcaro mansions lead Denver's June luxury home sales, each topping $8 million
stories.summary:
A five-bedroom Cedar Avenue mansion in Denver's Belcaro neighborhood claimed the top spot among June home sales in the metro area, selling for its $8.5 million list price. The 8,075-square-foot home, designed by BOSS Architecture in 2018, features a private elevator, Wolf appliances, and a pool and outdoor kitchen; it last sold for $7.9 million in 2022.

The second-priciest sale was a 12,769-square-foot English Country estate in the gated Polo Club enclave, which sold June 11 for 9% above its $7.5 million asking price, or roughly $8.175 million. The seller, private equity managing partner Alexander Wiegers, had previously sold a $10 million Cherry Hills penthouse. LIV Sotheby's International Realty broker Josh Behr represented both sides of the top two transactions.

The June data reflects an upper end of the Denver market that continues to transact at elevated prices even as the broader market softens and inventory climbs in mid-range segments.

stories.headline: April freeze wiped out entire fruit harvest for North Fork Valley orchards; growers scramble to survive without income
stories.summary:
An overnight freeze on April 17 that pushed temperatures into the low 20s destroyed the entire fruit crop across Colorado's North Fork Valley near Paonia, with most growers reporting a total loss. First Fruits, the 140-acre operation run by brothers Kevin and Kris Kropp, will harvest zero cherries, plums, peaches, nectarines, pears, apricots or apples this year — the first complete loss in nearly 50 years of farming for Kevin Kropp.

The North Fork Valley supplies roughly 10% of Colorado's total fruit harvest, with a concentration of organic orchards that serve farmers markets, CSA programs and wholesale buyers across the Western Slope and Front Range. The disaster is rippling outward to seasonal farmworkers, packing sheds and other businesses that depend on the harvest.

Growers are now confronting the challenge of maintaining irrigation, fertilization and employee payrolls through a year with no crop revenue. Several operations are pursuing alternative income streams and leaning on customer relationships built over years to bridge the gap until next season.

stories.headline: Westbound U.S. 36 in Westminster closed for hours after diesel fuel spill
stories.summary:
Westminster firefighters shut down westbound U.S. 36 between Pecos Street and Federal Boulevard at approximately 7:30 a.m. Saturday following a diesel fuel spill on the highway. The closure caused traffic disruptions in the north metro Denver corridor for several hours.

The Colorado Department of Transportation reported the highway had fully reopened by 2:22 p.m. No injuries were reported in connection with the spill. The Westminster Fire Department handled the hazardous-materials response.

weather.notable: Nearly 7 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I was the best man at the wedding. If I'm the best man, why is she marrying him?

topStory.headline: Sen. Lindsey Graham, R-S.C., Dies at 71 After Sudden Illness
topStory.summary:
U.S. Sen. Lindsey Graham, Republican of South Carolina and one of President Donald Trump's closest congressional allies, died Saturday evening at the age of 71. His office confirmed the death in a statement posted to social media, saying Graham passed away from a "brief and sudden illness" and asking the public to respect his family's privacy. Emergency scanner audio obtained by NBC News indicated that paramedics were dispatched to Graham's Capitol Hill home Saturday night for a cardiac arrest call, with CPR reported in progress.

Tributes poured in Sunday from political figures at home and abroad. President Trump described Graham on Truth Social as "one of the greatest people and Senators I have ever known" and called him "a true American Patriot." Senate Majority Leader John Thune said his "heart is heavy" over the loss of his friend and colleague, while South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster called Graham "the fiercest of fighters for South Carolina and America." Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said he and his wife were grieving alongside the American people, calling Graham "one of Israel's greatest friends," and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said he was "deeply saddened," noting that Graham had visited Ukraine ten times during the war and had met with him in Kyiv as recently as Friday, July 10.

Graham, born July 9, 1955, in Central, South Carolina, had a career in public service spanning more than three decades. He served as a military lawyer in the Air Force before entering politics, first winning a seat in the South Carolina House of Representatives, then the U.S. House, and finally the Senate in 2002, where he was reelected three additional times. He was chairman of the Senate Budget Committee at the time of his death and had secured his party's nomination for a fifth Senate term just weeks earlier, with a general election scheduled for November.

Graham was widely known as a hawkish voice on foreign policy, a strong advocate for NATO, and a persistent critic of Russia's war in Ukraine. A top staffer told NBC News there had been no indication Graham was feeling unwell before his death; he had been scheduled to appear on NBC's "Meet the Press" on Sunday, a program on which he had appeared 63 times. Under South Carolina law, Gov. McMaster will appoint a replacement to fill Graham's Senate seat through January 3, and the state Republican Party must select a new general election nominee, with a special primary expected by August 11.
